summary: map_openai_live_error maps OpenAiLiveError::WebSocket(tungstenite::Error) -> LlmError::NetworkTimeout. The error.to_string().contains("timed out") substring does NOT pick the outcome variant — the arm is unconditionally NetworkTimeout; the substring only toggles the informational duration_ms field (30_000 vs 0). Tracing the consumer: the recoverable-vs-fatal fork (meerkat-core/src/error.rs:446 is_recoverable, machine helper llm_failure_kind_recoverable at meerkat-machine-schema/.../meerkat_machine.rs:4788) keys purely on the typed LlmRetryFailureKind::NetworkTimeout VARIANT, never on duration_ms. The retry record (meerkat-core/src/retry.rs:53) and the LlmNetworkTimeout event carry duration_ms as telemetry only; no backoff/sleep/schedule computation reads it. So the substring drives no semantic decision in shared/runtime/surface logic. This is the legitimate parse-at-boundary site (provider crate translating a foreign tungstenite error into typed LlmError). It IS sloppy — tungstenite::Error::Io(io::Error) exposes io::ErrorKind::TimedOut, a typed discriminant that should be preferred over string matching — but that is a code-quality nit, not a Rule-4 authority violation. CONTESTED re-judge: prior refutation ('provider-internal NetworkTimeout parse') stands, for the stronger reason that the untyped carrier feeds only inert metadata, not a decision.
typed_owner: std::io::ErrorKind::TimedOut reachable via tungstenite::Error::Io (tungstenite-0.29 src/error.rs:41); typed outcome owner is LlmError::NetworkTimeout in meerkat-llm-core/src/error.rs:26 / LlmRetryFailureKind::NetworkTimeout in meerkat-core/src/retry.rs:18
repair_sketch: Optional quality improvement (not required by Rule 4): match the typed variant instead of stringifying — e.g. OpenAiLiveError::WebSocket(Error::Io(io)) if io.kind() == ErrorKind::TimedOut => NetworkTimeout{duration_ms: 30_000}, with other WebSocket errors mapping to ConnectionReset/StreamParseError. duration_ms remains cosmetic regardless since no decision reads it.

summary: RE-JUDGE (contested): NOT a violation. Line 571 branches on Option<&AuthBindingRef> in resolve_auth_binding_candidates_for_provider. Some = an explicit, fully-typed binding reference (realm/binding/profile are RealmId/BindingId/ProfileId newtypes) -> strict single-target resolution wrapped as vec![target]. None = no explicit binding supplied -> the shared best-available candidate-list policy (preferred realm -> default realm -> remaining realms -> env fallback). None has exactly ONE harmless meaning ('no explicit binding -> use default policy'); there is no second meaning of absence and no parallel bool encoding a missing third state. The identity is carried by a typed AuthBindingRef, not a string oracle (the docstring confirms the 'realm:binding[:profile]' string is split at the CLI boundary and reconstructed field-by-field). This is the legitimate explicit-or-default Option pattern, not hidden ownership. Confirms the prior audit's refutation; not part of any known cluster (M6 concerns Option<String> realm carriers, not this typed explicit-or-default branch).
repair_sketch: No repair needed; legitimate explicit-or-default Option<&AuthBindingRef>. Identity is already a typed newtype struct, and absence has a single harmless meaning.

summary: `if auth_binding.is_some()` at connection.rs:571 selects between strict single-target resolution and the ordered "best available" default-resolution policy. The carrier is `Option<&AuthBindingRef>`, where `AuthBindingRef` is a fully typed struct (RealmId + BindingId + Option<ProfileId>); Wave-b deliberately deleted its `parse`/`Display` so no `realm:binding` string form exists. Some = an explicit, strict, typed binding target was supplied; None = no explicit binding, run the default policy. Absence has exactly ONE harmless meaning ("explicit-or-default"). No parallel bool encodes a hidden third state (the separate `allow_env_default` flag is an orthogonal env-fallback gate, not a collapse of binding presence). The branch does not re-derive identity/role from an untyped representation — it merely picks strict-vs-default resolution over an already-typed value. This is the textbook legitimate explicit-or-default Option carve-out. Confirming the prior refutation.
typed_owner: AuthBindingRef (meerkat-core/src/connection.rs:129) — typed struct { realm: RealmId, binding: BindingId, profile: Option<ProfileId> }
repair_sketch: No repair needed; this is a legitimate explicit-or-default Option over an already-canonical typed binding ref. The decision driven by is_some() is strict-vs-default resolution, not re-derivation of meaning from an untyped carrier.

summary: CONTESTED re-judge -> CONFIRM NON-violation. Site is inside handle_tools_call_with_notifier (the top-level MCP tools/call dispatcher, lib.rs:1612). It matches on the wire tool_name: exact-string arms for individual tools, plus two prefix-guarded family routers (name.starts_with("meerkat_schedule_") at 1767 and name.starts_with("workgraph_") at 1776), ending in a closed fall-through `_ => Err(ToolCallError::method_not_found(...))` at 1824. The prefix does NOT recover hidden identity or drive a semantic decision; it selects a handler FAMILY. The forwarded full tool_name is then re-dispatched by exact-string match inside the owning crate (meerkat-schedule/src/tools.rs:223 handle_schedule_tools_call, match name { "meerkat_schedule_create" => ... }) which itself fails CLOSED at line 293 (`other => Err(ScheduleToolError::invalid_arguments(...))`), and the arguments are parsed into typed input structs at the boundary. This is the named legitimate exemption: transport/wire method dispatch by name that fails closed at the surface. The tool name IS the wire identity and is being dispatched, not parsed for concealed meaning. Not a Rule-4 violation and not part of any H1..L1 cluster.
typed_owner: N/A (legitimate carve-out): wire tool name dispatched via exact-string match families with closed fall-through; per-tool args parsed into typed inputs (e.g. ScheduleToolError fail-closed in meerkat-schedule/src/tools.rs:223)
repair_sketch: No repair needed. Optional cosmetic hardening only: the two prefix family-routers could be collapsed into a registry of (family-prefix -> handler) entries, but this would not change semantics and is not required by Rule 4 since the inner dispatch already fails closed on exact names.

summary: CONTESTED, re-judged NOT a violation. `auth_binding: Option<&AuthBindingRef>` at connection.rs:571 (and the sibling resolver at :440) is the legitimate single-meaning "explicit-or-default" Option pattern. `Some(ref)` -> resolve one strict typed target via resolve_auth_binding_or_default_for_provider; `None` -> apply the documented best-available candidate ordering (preferred realm, then `default` realm, then any realm, then env fallback). Absence has exactly ONE harmless meaning ("no explicit binding supplied"); there is no parallel bool collapsing a third state. `AuthBindingRef` is fully structural/typed (realm: RealmId, binding: BindingId, profile: Option<ProfileId>) with parse/Display deliberately deleted in Wave-b to prevent ferrying an opaque "realm:binding" join, so identity is already canonical. The only nearby `allow_env_default: bool` is an orthogonal capability axis, not a hidden tri-state of the binding, and `is_env_default()` is a typed predicate over newtype fields, not string recovery. No untyped carrier drives a semantic decision in shared logic.
typed_owner: AuthBindingRef (meerkat-core/src/connection.rs:129) with newtype fields RealmId/BindingId/ProfileId; the Option already encodes explicit-or-default cleanly
repair_sketch: None required. The carrier is already typed and the Option absence is single-meaning. If one wanted to make intent even more explicit one could introduce an enum BindingSelection { Explicit(AuthBindingRef), BestAvailable }, but this is cosmetic, not a Rule-4 obligation.

summary: resolve_auth_binding_candidates_for_provider branches on `auth_binding.is_some()` (line 571). The carrier is Option<&AuthBindingRef>, where AuthBindingRef is a fully-typed canonical identity struct (realm: RealmId, binding: BindingId, profile: Option<ProfileId>) — wave-b explicitly deleted its parse/Display so no opaque "realm:binding" join ferries through. The Option distinguishes exactly two states: Some = "explicit target, resolve strictly"; None = "no explicit binding, run best-available candidate-ordering policy". This is the textbook single-meaning explicit-or-default Option. No string/prefix/split recovers identity to drive the branch (.is_some() is a pure presence check on a typed value). The orthogonal allow_env_default: bool is a separate concern (env-fallback permission), not a third state of binding presence — there is no clear_binding/set_binding pair collapsing an Inherit/Set/Clear tri-state, and None never means "clear". All three callers (factory.rs:1454, factory.rs:2865, cli/main.rs:5886) pass Some(explicit) or None=default with no overloaded second meaning. Distinct from cluster M6, which is the Option<String> realm-carrier family; here the carrier is a typed struct, not a string. LEGITIMATE carve-out: Option<T> whose absence has exactly ONE harmless meaning. Prior audit refutation holds.
typed_owner: AuthBindingRef (meerkat-core/src/connection.rs:129) — already the canonical typed binding identity
repair_sketch: No repair needed. The Option<&AuthBindingRef> argument is a legitimate single-meaning explicit-or-default carrier over an already-canonical typed identity; no string oracle and no hidden tri-state.
